question: Define the following with respect to a rooted tree: (i) Parent (ii) Child (iii) Siblings (iv) Ancestor (v) Descendant
expectedPoints: Parent: Node that has one or more child nodes below it
Child: Node that has a parent node above it
Siblings: Nodes that share the same parent
Ancestor: Any node on the path from root to a given node
Descendant: Any node in the subtree rooted at a given node

question: Differentiate between: (i) Full binary tree and Complete binary tree (ii) Perfect binary tree and Balanced binary tree
expectedPoints: Full: Every node has 0 or 2 children | Complete: All levels filled except possibly last, nodes left-aligned
Perfect: All internal nodes have 2 children, all leaves at same level | Balanced: Height difference between left and right subtrees is at most 1

question: Explain the three methods of binary tree traversal with an example.
expectedPoints: Preorder (Root, Left, Right): Visit root first, then traverse left subtree, then right
Inorder (Left, Root, Right): Traverse left, visit root, traverse right
Postorder (Left, Right, Root): Traverse left, traverse right, visit root last

question: State and prove the relationship between number of leaf nodes and number of degree-2 internal nodes in a binary tree.
expectedPoints: Theorem: In any binary tree, number of leaf nodes = number of nodes with 2 children + 1 (L = I₂ + 1)
Proof using handshaking lemma: Sum of degrees = 2E, count edges as n-1, prove by induction or counting
This is useful for analyzing tree properties

question: Draw a binary tree with 10 nodes having minimum possible height. Also find its height.
expectedPoints: Minimum height tree is complete/perfect binary tree
For 10 nodes, minimum height = floor(log₂10) = 3 (levels 0,1,2,3)
Tree structure should show complete filling from left to right
Height = number of edges on longest path = 3

question: Define Binary Search Tree (BST). Insert the following sequence into an empty BST: 45, 25, 75, 15, 35, 65, 85. Show the final tree structure.
expectedPoints: BST: Left subtree contains smaller values, right subtree contains larger values
Insert 45 as root
25 < 45 goes left, 75 > 45 goes right
15 < 25 goes left, 35 > 25 goes right
65 < 75 goes left, 85 > 75 goes right
Final tree shows proper BST property maintained

question: Define the following: (i) Height of a tree (ii) Depth of a node (iii) Degree of a node (iv) Level of a node. For a binary tree with n nodes, prove that the maximum number of nodes at level k is 2^k.
expectedPoints: Height: Maximum depth (root to leaf path length)
Depth: Number of edges from root to node
Degree: Number of children a node has
Level: Depth of node + 1 (some texts use depth itself)
Proof: By induction - level 0 has 2^0=1 node, assume level k has at most 2^k, each can have max 2 children so level k+1 has max 2 × 2^k = 2^(k+1)

question:
Explain the tree traversal algorithms: Preorder, Inorder, and Postorder with pseudo code for each. For the tree below, give the traversal sequences:

 10
 / \
 5 15
 / \ \
 3 7 20
expectedPoints: Preorder (Root, Left, Right): Visit node, traverse left, traverse right | Code: if node != null: visit(node), preorder(left), preorder(right)
Inorder (Left, Root, Right): Traverse left, visit node, traverse right | Code: if node != null: inorder(left), visit(node), inorder(right)
Postorder (Left, Right, Root): Traverse left, traverse right, visit node | Code: if node != null: postorder(left), postorder(right), visit(node)
Answers: Preorder: 10 5 3 7 15 20 | Inorder: 3 5 7 10 15 20 | Postorder: 3 7 5 20 15 10

question: Define and differentiate between full, complete, and perfect binary trees. Also prove that a full binary tree with n internal nodes has exactly n+1 leaf nodes.
expectedPoints: Full: Each node has 0 or 2 children
Complete: All levels filled except possibly last, left-justified
Perfect: All internal nodes have 2 children, all leaves at same level

Proof: Use induction or count edges. For full binary tree with I internal nodes: Total nodes n = 2I + 1, leaf nodes L = I + 1 (since each internal node has 2 children edges, but each non-root internal node is a child, so edges = n - 1 = 2I = n - 1, solving gives L = I + 1)

question: What is a Binary Search Tree? Explain the algorithm to search an element in a BST. Also analyze the time complexity for searching in (i) Balanced BST (ii) Skewed BST.
expectedPoints: BST: Binary tree where left child < parent < right child for all nodes
Search algorithm: Compare target with current node, if equal found, if less go left, if greater go right, repeat until found or null
Balanced BST: O(log n) - height is log n, so at most log₂n comparisons
Skewed BST: O(n) - degenerates to linear search like linked list, height = n-1
